transcript: Yeah, this is Herbie Blunt. [exhales] I got a lottery ticket from y’all up ‘ere, uh, couple days ago.
And I won $200; was a big winner.
But I can’t find my ticket; I lost it. I lost my ticket. [exhales] So can you guys all just look me up and just verify it. I mean it came from your store, so…
I—there’s nothin’ I can help you with, on that, hon’.
Yes, I’m—I’m sorry, I mean. But there’s nothin’ I can do about it without the ticket—
—So you can just look up the code. I mean, it came from your store—
—No, I can’t look it up. No, you’d have to call the Lottery Commission to look up any tickets that you’ve lost.
Well, I did; they told me to talk to you all up here. I won $200!—
—Well, I don’t have—I don’t know how to go into this sys—this system! They’re full of it! Because I don’t know how to go in and look up old—old lottery tickets from three days ago!
Well, can you just give me $200? I mean, i’m in there two, three times a—
—No, I can’t give you any money without the ticket, no!—
—I’m in there three times a day!—
—And the Lottery Board knows that and they’ll tell you that!
I’m in there two, three times a day!
I know, but, hon’, without that ticket—I’m not giving you $200 without a ticket ‘cause then when it comes up short, then it comes outta my check! No, I’m not payin’ for it! I didn’t lose the ticket!—
—Just use your scanner! Just scan it in there and you’ll see that I got it from your store!
Y—well, you have to have the ticket for me to scan!—
—I don’t have the access—I don’t have access to get what you bought three days ago—
—And if you lost the ticket, that’s on you. I can’t give you $200 without the ticket!
Can you just give me some more tickets, then? Lottery tickets. $200 worth—
—No, I cannot give you $200 worth of lottery tickets in place for it, no! Uh-uh!—
—I already won! I won big time! $200—
—I—well, you didn’t—well, I don’t know that; you don’t have your ticket, so I can’t prove it!
[siren] Well, I’m tellin’ ya I did, didn’t I?
Well, yeah—that’s not enough for me, hon! Anybody could tell me that they’ve—they won $200 on a ticket. I—don’t mean I’m gon’ pay it!
Look, my grandfather came in there. He was in there two, three times a day!—
—Hon’ I’m sorry if your grandfather come in here and you come in here all the time. Without a ticket, I cannot do a payout!
My grandfather came before me, you understand?—
—I’m sorry, hon’, I got customers to tend to; I can’t stay on the phone!—
—Listen, just give me some tickets. I’ll pick up some tickets from you, alright? Later tonight—
—No, I’m not giving you any free tickets! Sorry!—
—Whadda ya mean? They’re not free; I already won from ya! Don’t ya see?—
—You haven’t won! You give me the money, you’ll get the tickets. Try to come up here and get the tickets without the money, you’ll see.
Country Store. May I help you?
Hey, this is Herbie Blunt callin’, [exhales] alright?
Uh, yes, I—I’m kinda busy, hon’. I cannot sit here and—and debate over whether I’m payin’ you $200 or not.
—Because without a ticket, I’m not doin’ it. Do what?
You’re gonna pay me; that’s—that’s not even—
—No, I’m not paying you anything! I tell you what, you come on up here and see if I’mmo give you free tickets or anything for free without a damn ticket to verify that you bought it!
—my grandfather used to come in there before me!—
—I don’t care if your grandfather came up here! I cannot give you anything for free! You don’t have a ticket, I cannot help you!
—Now stop callin’ and stop botherin’ me!
You’re harassin’ me, lady! A—you owe me $200—
—No, you’re callin’ here and harassin’ me! Now, like I said, if you wanna come on up here and try that, go right ahead! You—
—[siren] I know the guitar player Slāsh, and I’m gonna bring him in there and he—
—I don’t care who you know! I think you’re full o’ shit, is what I think!—
—He’s gonna come in there and whoop you!—
—Bring your chickenshit ass on up here, then!
Hello? This is Herbie Blunt callin’. [siren] Now listen, I need y’all to fetch what I need, okay? That’s alls I need today, is my 200 lottery ticket, alright?
Well, you are gonna have to contact the Lottery Commission. We don’t have anything to do with that—
—Well then, if you’ve talked to them, they can send you your money. When they tell you—well—
—Well they told me to talk to the manager up here, is who I need to talk to.
No, she’s not allowed to give you the money. She’s talked to the owners and they will not allow that.
Well, I’m gonna come up there and get what I need! So—
—Well, if you do that, you’re gonna get what you need and probably a bullet in your ass at the same time, so you go right ahead—
—Hey, I’m gonna come and body blow! Body blow! Body blow! How’s that sound?
Okay, well that sounds great! You come right on up, sweetheart; we’re waitin’—
—Hey, I know Slash, you ever heard of Slāsh? The guitar player?—

—I—You know what? I know all kinds ‘o people; I know good meth-heads and bad meth-heads and, you know, I know all kinda ‘o things, too. But, if you think that you’re gonna come up here and get your lottery ticket, then you march your li’l happy ass right on up here.
Slāsh is gonna come up ‘ere and whoop ya! You got me?
Well, that’s good! Alright, we’ll send him home.
Hey, I’mma come up there and body blow! Body blow! Body blow! Does that sound good?—
[siren] Why don’t you just fetch what I need?
I don’t know how to fetch; I’m not a fuckin’ dog.
Just fetch me my money; that’s alls I need!
Well, go fetch a fuckin’ job and quit playin’ lottery!
Hey, I’ve got both your numbers; I’ll have the owners call you back when they’re ready—
—I’ll bring my machete up there; what do you think of that?—
—Why don’t you bring your machete up? You’re the same stupid ass threatenin’ to come up here with a tree limb and beat my ass! Come on and do it!—
—Quit talkin’ your shit! Old man, ain’t you got nothin’ better to do?—
—Get your dick outta the dirt and do somethin’, you chickenshit motherfucker! Come on!
[Ayla, an employee of a lottery corporation, is present]
Lewis Country Store. May I help you?
Hello! This is Ayla at the lottery. What can I do for ya? You had some questions about sellin’ a winnin’ ticket. I dunno—
Oh! It’s a—no, it is a man who is callin’ and harassin’ us—
—and he wanted me to pay him for a ticket he had bought that was a $200 winner, he said, and I told him no, I couldn’t do it without the ticket—
—And he told me that yes, I could, that y’all told him I could—
—And I told him no. And so, yeah, that’s who called you. He’s just tryin’ to harass us more—
—If I were you, I would—yeah, I wouldn’t listen to anybody else—
—I guess he just transferred me over to you. That—I don’t—I don’t know why, but [chuckles] okay!—
—I don’t know how he done it or whatever—
—But yeah, he’s the same—it’s the same crappy old man that was callin’ up here last night harassin’ us about the tickets. So yeah—
[Tammy, an employee of the Louisiana Lottery Corporation, is present]
Hi, this is Tammy. Can I help you? Hello?—
Oh! No, I didn’t call you; I spoke to somebody, um, I guess, from your store—
—No, you definitely called me; I just answered the phone—
—Okay. I didn’t call you. This is Tamm—
—and it says, “Private” on my caller ID.
This is Tammy from the Louisiana Lottery; I did not call you; someone just patched in—
—Okay. Look, sweetheart, y’all—y’all—quit callin’ me, okay?—
—Quit callin’ me. This is the third time y’all’ve called me. Stop it. We’re not even in Louisiana; we’re in Tennessee. So stop callin’ me!
[Michael, an employee of a lottery corporation, is present]
Hi! This is Michael at the lottery! You have questions?
No, this isn’t Michael with the lottery. This is area code 971–

—276-2889. And let me tell you somethin’, you call up my goddamn employees again, I will hunt you down and I’ll make you wish you had a goddamn machete. Do you understand me? You quit callin’ my goddamn business! I’m gonna have your goddamn ass arrested. I’ve got your goddamn caller ID—
